content.text: Not all AI agents carry equal risk. A code documentation assistant that generates comments for internal developers carries fundamentally different governance implications than an agent that makes credit decisions affecting thousands of applicants each month. Risk classification — the systematic assignment of agents to risk tiers based on defined criteria — determines the governance requirements that apply: which documentation is required, what oversight mechanisms must be in place, who must approve deployment, and how frequently the agent must be reviewed. Without a risk classification framework, governance requirements are applied inconsistently: over-governed for low-risk systems (wasting resources and slowing innovation) and under-governed for high-risk systems (creating liability and harm).

This sub-subtopic establishes a four-tier classification framework for AI agents, defines the factors that drive classification decisions, maps each tier to specific governance requirements, and applies EU AI Act high-risk categories to the agent context. The framework draws on multiple regulatory sources (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001 risk assessment requirements) to provide a classification system that satisfies real regulatory obligations rather than just internal preferences.

attrs.alt: Agent risk classification showing four tiers from Tier 4 Critical Risk through Tier 1 Low Risk with governance requirements, with classification factors including automation degree, decision stakes, affected population, regulatory context, and reversibility

content.text: A flat governance approach — same requirements for all agents — is inefficient and ultimately counterproductive. When every agent requires a full algorithmic impact assessment, quarterly board review, and third-party audit, teams will route around governance to avoid the overhead, deploying agents as 'experiments' or 'pilots' that never formally enter the compliance framework. A tiered approach makes governance proportionate to risk, which makes compliance feasible and therefore more likely to actually happen.

Risk classification also creates a clear language for conversations between governance teams and product teams. Rather than negotiating governance requirements from scratch for each agent, the product team knows: 'if this agent is Tier 3, these are the requirements; if we can design it to be Tier 2, these are the requirements.' This shifts governance discussion from 'do we have to do this?' to 'what tier should we target?' — a much more productive frame.
Proportionate governance: high-risk agents receive rigorous oversight; low-risk agents receive lightweight oversight without unnecessary friction.
Clear language for product-compliance negotiation: tier classification defines requirements without per-agent negotiation.
Consistent decision-making: the same factors drive classification for every agent, preventing inconsistent treatment across teams or product lines.
Regulatory alignment: tier definitions incorporate EU AI Act high-risk categories, NIST AI RMF impact levels, and ISO 42001 risk criteria.
Portfolio visibility: a classified agent registry shows the organization's risk profile at a glance.
Classification edge cases require judgment: an agent that spans multiple tiers (high automation but low decision stakes) requires a documented rationale rather than algorithmic assignment.
Tier creep — the tendency to classify agents in lower tiers to avoid governance overhead — must be actively managed through independent classification review.

Regulatory frameworks may override the internal tier system: an agent classified Tier 2 internally may still be EU AI Act high-risk, which triggers regulatory requirements regardless of internal classification.

How autonomous is the agent? An agent that only retrieves information and presents it for human decision carries lower risk than an agent that retrieves information and automatically acts on it. Key questions: does the agent make final decisions or does a human review before action? Does the agent take external actions (sending communications, modifying records) autonomously? Is there a human escalation path for uncertain cases?

How significant are the consequences of the agent's decisions for affected individuals? A decision that denies a loan application, affects employment, determines insurance coverage, or influences medical treatment has high stakes. A decision that recommends a marketing email subject line or categorizes an internal support ticket has low stakes. Stakes are assessed by the severity and reversibility of harm if the agent makes an incorrect decision.

Who is affected, how vulnerable are they, and at what scale? An agent used by sophisticated internal engineers who can detect and correct errors is lower risk than an agent making decisions about economically vulnerable consumers who may lack recourse. Scale matters: an agent processing 100 transactions per year carries lower aggregate risk than one processing 100,000 per month, even if each individual decision has the same stakes.

Does the agent operate in a regulated sector with specific AI governance requirements? Financial services (ECOA, FCRA, OCC model risk), healthcare (FDA SaMD, HIPAA), employment (EEOC, state screening laws), and education (FERPA, COPPA) all impose sector-specific requirements that may force higher tier classification independent of the other factors. EU AI Act high-risk categories (Annex III) are particularly important: if an agent falls within these categories, Tier 4 governance is the minimum regardless of internal classification.

Can the consequences of an incorrect AI decision be undone? A incorrectly generated support ticket can be edited. An incorrectly denied loan application requires an appeal process. An incorrectly screened-out job applicant cannot recover the missed opportunity. Irreversibility is the factor most frequently underweighted: teams focus on severity and likelihood but underestimate the compounding harm of irreversible decisions made at scale.
EU AI Act High-Risk Categories Applied to Agents

The EU AI Act Annex III lists eight high-risk AI system categories. These are mandatory high-risk classifications regardless of the deployer's internal risk framework. Agents that fall within these categories must satisfy EU AI Act Title III requirements (conformity assessment, registration, technical documentation, human oversight, accuracy and robustness standards).
Grid management agents, network security agents
AI grading agents, student performance agents
Resume screening agents, performance review agents
Access to essential private services and public services
Credit scoring agents, benefits eligibility agents
Visa screening agents, risk assessment agents
Legal research agents, sentencing support agents
Mental Model: Speed Limits on Different Roads

Risk classification is like the speed limit system on roads. A residential street has a 25 mph limit — appropriate for the level of activity and vulnerability of pedestrians nearby. A highway has a 65 mph limit — appropriate for controlled-access infrastructure with separated traffic. A school zone drops to 15 mph when children are present, regardless of road type. Applying the same governance requirements to all AI agents regardless of risk is like applying a highway speed limit to a school zone: legal on paper, disastrous in practice. The risk classification system defines the appropriate governance 'speed' for each agent context.

An agent is Tier 1 if it: operates only on internal data; has no external effects; makes no consequential decisions about individuals; has low data sensitivity; and operates in a non-regulated context. Examples: a code documentation agent, an internal Q&A chatbot over company policies, a log analysis agent for internal debugging. Governance requirements: basic documentation (system card, tool list), team-level review before deployment, compliance notification for registry. No formal AIA required. Annual lightweight review.

An agent is Tier 2 if it: automates business processes with moderate data sensitivity; makes decisions that affect individuals but with a clear and accessible remediation path; operates in an environment where errors can be detected and corrected within a reasonable time. Examples: a customer support chatbot that creates tickets and retrieves account information, an internal workflow automation agent, a content moderation agent with human review. Governance requirements: lightweight AIA, internal audit coverage, manager-level deployment approval, human-on-loop monitoring, annual review.

An agent is Tier 3 if it: makes consequential decisions that directly affect individuals in ways that are difficult to reverse; operates in a regulated sector; processes sensitive personal data at scale; or takes external actions with significant organizational or individual consequences. Examples: a customer communications agent sending regulated notices, an HR screening support tool, a fraud detection agent triggering account actions. Governance requirements: full AIA, regular internal audit, executive-level deployment sign-off, human-in-loop for critical decisions, semi-annual review.

An agent is Tier 4 if it: makes or materially influences irreversible decisions with high individual stakes; falls within EU AI Act high-risk categories; operates in critical infrastructure or safety-critical contexts; or manages financial settlement at significant scale. Examples: an automated credit underwriting agent, a clinical decision support agent used in treatment decisions, a sentencing support agent in criminal justice, an energy grid management agent. Governance requirements: full AIA, full AI management system (ISO 42001-aligned), third-party independent audit, board-level deployment sign-off, human approval for every consequential action, quarterly review.

"""
agent_risk_classifier.py
Classifies AI agents into risk tiers based on defined criteria.
Produces a documented classification with rationale.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional
import json

@dataclass
class ClassificationInput:
    agent_name: str
    description: str
    # Factor 1: Automation degree
    makes_final_decisions_autonomously: bool
    takes_external_actions_autonomously: bool
    human_escalation_path_exists: bool
    # Factor 2: Decision stakes
    decision_type: str    # "no_individual_decision", "low_stakes", "medium_stakes", "high_stakes"
    decision_reversibility: str  # "easily_reversible", "hard_to_reverse", "irreversible"
    # Factor 3: Affected population
    affected_population_type: str  # "internal_only", "business_users", "general_consumers", "vulnerable_groups"
    monthly_decision_volume: int
    # Factor 4: Regulatory context
    regulated_sector: bool
    eu_ai_act_high_risk_annex_iii: bool
    ecoa_or_fair_lending_scope: bool
    hipaa_scope: bool
    employment_law_scope: bool
    # Factor 5: Reversibility of harm
    harm_reversibility: str  # "reversible", "partially_reversible", "irreversible"

TIER_DESCRIPTIONS = {
    4: "Critical Risk",
    3: "High Risk",
    2: "Medium Risk",
    1: "Low Risk",
}

GOVERNANCE_REQUIREMENTS = {
    4: [
        "Full Algorithmic Impact Assessment",
        "AI Management System (ISO 42001-aligned)",
        "Third-party independent audit annually",
        "Board-level deployment sign-off",
        "Human approval required for every consequential action",
        "Quarterly review with executive reporting",
        "EU AI Act conformity assessment if EU-market",
    ],
    3: [
        "Full Algorithmic Impact Assessment",
        "Regular internal audit coverage",
        "Executive-level deployment sign-off",
        "Human-in-loop for all critical decisions",
        "Semi-annual review",
        "Incident notification to compliance team within 24 hours",
    ],
    2: [
        "Lightweight impact assessment",
        "Manager-level deployment approval",
        "Human-on-loop monitoring",
        "Annual review",
        "Incident reporting to compliance team within 5 days",
    ],
    1: [
        "Basic documentation (system card, tool list)",
        "Team-level review",
        "Compliance notification for registry",
        "Annual lightweight review",
    ],
}

def classify_agent(inp: ClassificationInput) -> dict:
    tier = 1
    rationale = []

    # EU AI Act Annex III is an automatic Tier 4 trigger
    if inp.eu_ai_act_high_risk_annex_iii:
        tier = max(tier, 4)
        rationale.append("EU AI Act Annex III high-risk category applies — minimum Tier 4")

    # Autonomy factors
    if inp.makes_final_decisions_autonomously and inp.takes_external_actions_autonomously:
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("Fully autonomous decision + external action = Tier 3 minimum")

    # Decision stakes
    if inp.decision_type == "high_stakes":
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("High-stakes individual decisions = Tier 3 minimum")
    elif inp.decision_type == "medium_stakes":
        tier = max(tier, 2)
        rationale.append("Medium-stakes decisions = Tier 2 minimum")

    # Reversibility
    if inp.harm_reversibility == "irreversible" and inp.decision_type in ("high_stakes", "medium_stakes"):
        tier = max(tier, 4)
        rationale.append("Irreversible high-stakes harm = Tier 4 minimum")
    elif inp.harm_reversibility == "partially_reversible" and inp.decision_type == "high_stakes":
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("Partially reversible high-stakes harm = Tier 3 minimum")

    # Affected population vulnerability
    if inp.affected_population_type == "vulnerable_groups":
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("Vulnerable population affected = Tier 3 minimum")
    elif inp.affected_population_type == "general_consumers" and inp.monthly_decision_volume > 10000:
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("Large consumer-facing decision volume = Tier 3 minimum")

    # Regulatory context
    if inp.ecoa_or_fair_lending_scope or inp.hipaa_scope or inp.employment_law_scope:
        tier = max(tier, 3)
        rationale.append("Sector-specific regulatory scope (ECOA/HIPAA/Employment) = Tier 3 minimum")

    return {
        "agent_name": inp.agent_name,
        "classification_date": str(date.today()),
        "tier": tier,
        "tier_name": TIER_DESCRIPTIONS[tier],
        "rationale": rationale,
        "governance_requirements": GOVERNANCE_REQUIREMENTS[tier],
        "next_review_months": {4: 3, 3: 6, 2: 12, 1: 12}[tier],
    }

# Examples
agents = [
    ClassificationInput(
        agent_name="Credit Underwriting Agent",
        description="Automated consumer credit decisions",
        makes_final_decisions_autonomously=True,
        takes_external_actions_autonomously=True,
        human_escalation_path_exists=True,
        decision_type="high_stakes",
        decision_reversibility="hard_to_reverse",
        affected_population_type="general_consumers",
        monthly_decision_volume=5000,
        regulated_sector=True,
        eu_ai_act_high_risk_annex_iii=True,
        ecoa_or_fair_lending_scope=True,
        hipaa_scope=False,
        employment_law_scope=False,
        harm_reversibility="partially_reversible",
    ),
    ClassificationInput(
        agent_name="Internal Code Documentation Agent",
        description="Generates code comments for internal developers",
        makes_final_decisions_autonomously=False,
        takes_external_actions_autonomously=False,
        human_escalation_path_exists=True,
        decision_type="no_individual_decision",
        decision_reversibility="easily_reversible",
        affected_population_type="internal_only",
        monthly_decision_volume=500,
        regulated_sector=False,
        eu_ai_act_high_risk_annex_iii=False,
        ecoa_or_fair_lending_scope=False,
        hipaa_scope=False,
        employment_law_scope=False,
        harm_reversibility="reversible",
    ),
]

for agent in agents:
    result = classify_agent(agent)
    print(f"\n=== {result['agent_name']} ===")
    print(f"Tier: {result['tier']} — {result['tier_name']}")
    print(f"Rationale: {'; '.join(result['rationale'])}")
    print(f"Governance requirements: {len(result['governance_requirements'])} items")
    print(f"Next review: {result['next_review_months']} months")
Real-World Example: HR Screening Agent Classification

A technology company built an AI agent to assist with resume screening. Initial discussions classified it as 'internal tooling' (Tier 1) because it was used by internal HR staff rather than directly by applicants. When the AIA process was applied, the team recognized that the agent was making consequential employment decisions for external job applicants — a population that is protected under employment law and lacks any visibility into or control over the AI screening process. Employment law scope, the high-stakes nature of employment decisions, and the irreversibility of a missed opportunity (once a candidate is screened out, they are gone) drove the classification to Tier 3. The governance requirements at Tier 3 — full AIA, executive deployment sign-off, human review of all AI-driven rejections in the first year, and a formal bias audit — would not have been applied if the initial 'internal tooling' classification had stood unchallenged.
Classifying based on who uses the agent rather than who is affected — an internally operated agent making consequential decisions about external individuals is high-risk regardless of internal use.
Using the deployment framing ('it's just a pilot') to avoid classification — an agent in a 'pilot' that makes real consequential decisions is not low-risk because it is labeled a pilot.
Ignoring EU AI Act Annex III categories — if an agent falls within a listed category, Tier 4 is the minimum regardless of internal classification.
Not reviewing classification when the agent's scope changes — an agent that starts internal and is later deployed to make consumer-facing decisions requires reclassification.
Treating reversibility as binary — there is a meaningful governance difference between an action that is reversible with significant effort and one that is easily undone.
Build the classification decision into the AI system intake process — every new agent should be classified before deployment approval is requested.
Require an independent reviewer to validate the classification — the product team proposing the agent has incentive to underclassify; governance or risk teams should provide independent review.
Document the rationale for classification decisions, including why higher tiers were not selected — audit-defensible classification decisions require written reasoning.
Review classification when the agent's scope, affected population, or decision type changes — classification is not a one-time deployment-gate decision.
Maintain a classified agent registry so the organization's aggregate risk profile is visible to the compliance and executive team.
For EU AI Act compliance, map your internal tiers to the Act's prohibited, high-risk, and limited-risk categories explicitly.

The classification process must be fast enough that it does not become a deployment bottleneck for low-risk agents. A two-question screening (Does this agent make consequential decisions about individuals? Is it in scope for EU AI Act Annex III?) should be resolvable in minutes. If both answers are no, a Tier 1 or 2 classification is likely and the full classification questionnaire can be completed by the product team without governance team involvement. Only Tier 3 and 4 classifications require governance team review, keeping the load manageable as the agent portfolio scales.

The classified agent registry must be accurate to be a governance tool. Regular sweeps to verify that all deployed agents are registered and that their classifications remain current are necessary. An unregistered agent — one deployed without going through the classification and approval process — is itself a compliance failure. Include agent registration as a required step in the deployment pipeline, and implement monitoring to detect agents calling production APIs that are not in the registry.
Risk classification assigns agents to governance tiers based on five factors: automation degree, decision stakes, affected population, regulatory context, and reversibility of harm.
Tier 4 (Critical Risk) requires full AIA, third-party audit, board sign-off, and human approval for every consequential action; Tier 1 (Low Risk) requires basic documentation and team review.
EU AI Act Annex III categories are automatic Tier 4 triggers regardless of internal risk assessment — any agent within these categories must satisfy EU AI Act high-risk requirements.
Classification should be based on who is affected by the agent, not who operates it — internal HR tools making employment decisions about external applicants are high-risk.
The classification decision must be documented with rationale for the tier assigned and documented reasons why higher tiers were not selected.
Classification must be reviewed when scope, affected population, or decision type changes — deployment-gate classification alone is insufficient.
An independent reviewer should validate classifications proposed by the product team to prevent systematic underclassification driven by governance-avoidance incentives.
